elements.sent-2411-001: The method relies on conditional probability densities to describe information about both data and parameters, yet the notion of conditional densities is inadmissible: probabilities of the same physical event, computed from conditional densities under different parameterizations, may be inconsistent.
elements.sent-2411-002: We show that this inconsistency, together with acausality in hierarchical methods, invalidate a variety of commonly applied Bayesian methods when applied to problems in the physical world, including trans-dimensional inference, general Bayesian dimensionality reduction methods, and hierarchical and empirical Bayes.
elements.sent-2411-003: Models in parameter spaces of different dimensionalities cannot be compared -- invalidating the concept of natural parsimony, the probabilistic counterpart to Occams Razor.
elements.sent-2411-004: Bayes theorem itself is inadmissible, and Bayesian inference applied to parameters that characterize physical properties requires reformulation.

elements.sent-2411-006: This has turned out to be immensely useful in scientific inference, where information from independent sources are combined to obtain probabilistic results.

elements.sent-2411-010: However, when applied to inference problems involving physical quantities, where probability densities over multi-dimensional manifolds are sought, a serious flaw in the formalism appears.
elements.sent-2411-011: It was discovered already by Bertrand (1889), and later confirmed by Borel (1909) and Kolmogorov (1933), that conditional probability densities over multi-dimensional manifolds are, in fact, inadmissible.
elements.sent-2411-012: In practice, this means that conditional probability densities, and hence Bayesian inference, are not invariant under coordinate/parameter changes.

elements.section-2411-001: Bayesian Model Selection through Evidences and Bayes Factors
elements.sent-2411-052: A Bayesian model selection problem is one where structural or statistical parameters $\bfh$, usually termed {\em hyperparameters , instead of being given beforehand, are included in the set of unknown variables: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} p_{m,h|d}(\bfm^{(\bfh)},\bfh|\bfd)) = \frac{p_{d|m,h}(\bfd|\bfm^{(\bfh)},\bfh)p_{m|h}(\bfm^{(\bfh)}|\bfh)p_h(\bfh)}{p_d(\bfd)} = \frac{p_{d}(g_h(\bfm^{(\bfh)}))p_{m|h}(\bfm^{(\bfh)}|\bfh)p_k(\bfh)}{p_d(\bfd)}\ . \end{aligned} \label{eq: Bayesian-model-select}\end{equation} Here, $\bfh$ may include discrete parameters, such as the dimensionality of parameter space as in a later section on {\em trans-dimensional inversion , or an index referring to different parameterizations with the same dimensionality.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Alternatively, $\bfh$ consists of continuous parameters, such as the variances of data uncertainties or physical model priors, as in our discussion of {\em hierarchical Bayes .}
elements.sent-2411-054: The distributions $p_d$, $p_h$, $p_{m|h}$, $p_{m,h|d}$ and $p_{d|m,h}$ are marginals and conditionals derived from the joint prior probability $p_{d,m,h}$.
elements.sent-2411-055: We have also assumed that all of the prior probability distributions $p_d$, $p_{m|h}$ and $p_h$ are independent.
elements.sent-2411-056: According to Formulation 2 of Bayesian inversion presented above, $p_{d (g_h(\bfm^{(\bfh) ))$ is the likelihood and $p_{m|h (\bfm^{(\bfh) |\bfh)$ is the prior, both now conditioned on $\bfh$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: In this context, $p_d(\bfd)$ is termed the {\em total evidence : \begin{equation}p_d(\bfd) = \sum_{\hat{\bfh}} \int p_{d|h}(\bfd | \bfh) p_h(\bfh) d\tilde{\bfh}\end{equation} where $\bfh = (\hat{\bfh ,\tilde{\bfh )$ may be divided into a discrete part $\hat{\bfh}$ and a continuous part $\tilde{\bfh}$, and where the individual {\em evidences (or {\em marginal likelihoods ) for each value of $\bfh$ are given by \begin{equation}p_{d|h}(\bfd | \bfh) = \int_{\calM} p_d(g_k(\bfm^{(\bfh)}))p_{m|h}(\bfm^{(\bfh)}|\bfh) \ d\bfm^{(\bfh)} \ . \label{eq:evidence}\end{equation} Bayesian model selection is used to compute the posterior probability distribution in the joint $(\bfm^{(\bfh) ,\bfh)$-space, allowing comparison of posterior probabilities, not only amongst models with the same hyperparameters, but also across models with different hyperparameters.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: In this method it is claimed that the evidence $p_{d|h (\bfd | \bfh)$ measures how well the hypothesis that our model with hyperparameters $\bfh$ explains the data (under the given prior information).}
elements.sent-2411-059: A common measure of how favorable a hypothesis (a value of) $\bfh'$ is, compared to a hypothesis $\bfh''$, is the ratio of posterior probabilities of $\bfh$: \begin{equation}\frac{p_{h|d}(\bfh' | \bfd)}{p_{h|d}(\bfh'' | \bfd)} = \frac{p_d(\bfd | \bfh')}{p_d(\bfd | \bfh'')} \frac{p_h(\bfh')}{p_h(\bfh'')}\ ,\end{equation} where the first ratio on the right-hand-side is the so-called {\em Bayes Factor , the ratio of the evidences of $\bfh'$ and $\bfh''$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The more this factor increases, the more we prefer $\bfh'$ over $\bfh''$ given the data $\bfd$.
elements.sent-2411-061: Clearly, the evidence plays a key role in Bayesian model selection, since it decides which models are favorable.
elements.sent-2411-062: We now investigate the BK-inconsistency \cite{Bertrand1889,Borel1909,Kolmogorov1933 and its consequences for Bayesian inversion of physical data in general, and Bayesian model selection in particular.}

elements.section-2411-003: Inconsistency of Bayesian Model Selection Methods
elements.sent-2411-101: We now turn our attention to the consequences of the BK-inconsistency in the methods of hierarchical and empirical Bayes.
elements.sent-2411-102: Consider an inverse problem where the noise distribution of data, and/or the a priori distribution of model parameters, are unknown.
elements.sent-2411-103: In a standard formulation of Bayesian inversion this would render the problem unsolvable, but the methods of {\em hierarchical Bayes \cite{Casella1985,Gelman1997 , and its close relative, {\em empirical Bayes \cite{Good1956,Robbins1956,Carlin2000 , apparently offer a way out: Let $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ be a vector of parameter(s) that define the model prior (e.g., means and standard deviations for a fixed family of distributions), and the data/noise uncertainty (e.g., means and standard deviations of measurements).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: Hierarchical Bayes now uses equation \ref{eq: Bayesian-model-select with $\bfh = \boldsymbol{\theta}$.}
elements.sent-2411-105: As we show below, there is a fundamental problem with hierarchical Bayes, rooted in the BK-inconsistency: Consider an inverse problem, where we are unable to fit the data with the chosen parameterization and the available forward relation.
elements.sent-2411-106: The reason for this may be `noise', defined as the data residual after data fitting.
elements.sent-2411-107: In this example our problem is overdetermined, which means that the image of the model space (in data space) under the forward function has lower dimensionality than the data space.
elements.sent-2411-108: Consequently, the evidence needed to calculate hyperparameters is an integral over the data distribution, conditioned on a low-dimensional subspace in the data space.
elements.sent-2411-109: This evidence calculation can be made over exactly the same data, and with exactly the same uncertainties, but under different data parameterizations.

elements.subsection-2411-005: Conclusion and Discussion of the impact of the BK-inconsistency on Bayesian Model Selection
elements.sent-2411-156: The above result forces us to conclude that standard Bayesian theory does not allow useful comparison between different data- and model priors, and between models of different dimensionalities.
elements.sent-2411-157: Bayesian model selection methods are based on the idea that lack of knowledge about prior data and parameter uncertainties, or incomparability of probabilities between model spaces of different dimensionalities, can be offset by directing our attention to the data fits (evidences).
elements.sent-2411-158: Calculation of evidences involves an integration over the model spaces, thereby apparently removing the differences in dimensionalities, either in the model space, or in its image in the data space.
elements.sent-2411-159: However, as we have seen above, this is not the case.
elements.sent-2411-160: The BK-inconsistency sets in when evidences are calculated in any overdetermined case resulting from either a simplified forward function (as in many applications of hierarchical Bayes), or from the use of sparse parameterizations (as in trans-dimensional inversion).
elements.sent-2411-161: In the latter case, the concept of natural parsimony is rendered invalid.
elements.sent-2411-162: In the literature, there are a number of examples of physical problems which indicate that parsimony is not natural.
elements.sent-2411-163: In a recent work on trans-dimensional electrical resistivity tomography using the reversible-Jump Monte Carlo algorithm \cite{Galetti2018 it was found that natural parsimony was not achieved for certain choices of step size in the model proposal when changing dimensionality.}
elements.sent-2411-164: Furthermore, in a study of trans-dimensional inversion of surface waves \cite{Zhang2018 a clear dependence of the calculated model complexity (number of parameters) on the choice of parameterization of the subsurface was found.}
elements.sent-2411-165: These observations comprised important motivations for our investigation, and are both aligned with the findings of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-166: Our findings also have far-reaching implications for comparisons between different, overlapping, but incompatible model parameterizations.
elements.sent-2411-167: In current Bayesian formulations the only way to compare such parameterizations is through evidence calculations.
elements.sent-2411-168: Our results show that such evidences are not uniquely defined when applied to problems in physical sciences.
elements.subsection-2411-006: Logical Acausality of Hierarchical and Empirical Bayes
elements.sent-2411-169: In hierarchical Bayes we have augmented the usual unknown parameters $\bfm$ with hyper-parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, parameterizing the prior distribution in the data space and in the parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-170: In cases where $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is unknown, this version of Bayes theorem offers a way to estimate them from the information in data and in the forward function.
elements.sent-2411-171: This formulation of Bayesian inversion is based on the assumption that there exists a {\em true (independent of the analyst), but unknown distribution $p_{m|\theta (\bfm|\boldsymbol{\theta )$ belonging to a parameterized family of distributions which can take the role of prior in the calculation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: The parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ defining this prior are themselves considered random with distributions (the so-called {\em hyper priors ) supplied by the analyst(s).}
elements.sent-2411-173: The rationale is that, in the classical Bayes method, the prior may be difficult to define by the user, and different users may suggest different priors and obtain different results.
elements.sent-2411-174: The thinking is therefore that there is a randomness in the population of analysts that can be modeled using the hyper-parameters, and if we can provide a prior distribution for the hyper-parameters, we can find their posterior distribution through Bayesian data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-175: In other words, the random population of analysts have an uncertain idea of the `true prior', but this can be improved by the introduction of data.
elements.sent-2411-176: This basic assumption of hierarchical and empirical Bayes, when applied to inverse problems, contradicts our fundamental Assumption A that the prior is {\em input to the calculation, originating from the analyst(s).}
elements.sent-2411-177: It is true that there is a new (hyper) prior $p_{\theta (\boldsymbol{\theta )$ to be provided by the analyst(s), but this prior is not about a physical parameter, and is therefore hardly informed by any empirical knowledge -- or even intuition -- as a regular prior on data or model parameters would be.}}
elements.sent-2411-178: The above considerations may seem purely philosophical, but as we show in Appendix \ref{Appendix: Simple-Hierarch-B , the deviant assumptions in hierarchical and empirical Bayes have concrete mathematical consequences for the analysis.}

elements.sent-2411-246: Imagine that we have analyzed laboratory data, and solved an inverse problem leading to a solution for temperature $T$ and density $\rho$ of a gas in a confined volume.

elements.sent-2411-248: It can be shown that the maximum point for this distribution (the ``best-fitting solution'' to the inverse problem) is: \begin{equation}(T,\rho)_{max} = \left(\exp\left(\mu_T-\sigma_T^2\right),\exp\left(\mu_\rho-\sigma_\rho^2\right)\right).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-292: The evidence for $k = 1$ therefore becomes: \begin{equation}p(\bfd_{obs}|1) = \frac{1}{(2\sigma)^3} \, L \, \frac{1}{\Delta m} \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-293: We can now compute the Bayes factor to be used in the selection of $k$: \begin{equation}B = \frac{p(\bfd_{obs}|2)}{p(\bfd_{obs}|1)} = \frac{2\sigma (d_2 - 2d_1 + 3\sigma)}{{\Delta m}\, L} = 2,13333333\dots \label{eq: Bayes-fac-Cart-A}\end{equation} favoring $k = 2$.

elements.sent-2411-366: Provide enough information to put your work in context, but do not attempt a comprehensive review.
elements.sent-2411-367: General guidance on \LaTeX: The Science-family journals accept papers written in \LaTeX, but they are a minority of the submissions we receive.
elements.sent-2411-368: Our production department does not handle \LaTeX\ directly, instead we use conversion software to automatically process the .tex file into a format they can use.
elements.sent-2411-369: That works well provided the .tex file is straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-370: Keep it simple and follow this template.
elements.sent-2411-371: Don't import additional packages or define complex new commands.
elements.sent-2411-372: Figures and tables: These should be inserted at the end of the main text, as below (not in the middle of the text).
elements.sent-2411-373: Refer to them using e.g.~Figure~\ref{fig:example (or Fig.~\ref{fig:example ) and Table~\ref{tab:example .}}}
elements.sent-2411-374: Citing references: Science uses a numeric citation system.
elements.sent-2411-376: The template will combine reference numbers automatically~\cite{example,example2 , including ranges~\cite{example,example2, example_preprint .}}
elements.sent-2411-377: Reference author names and years should be stated in the reference list, not in the text.
elements.sent-2411-378: If you want to add a comment, use the syntax [see \cite{example for details].}
elements.sent-2411-379: Referring to supplementary material: Whenever more details are given in the Materials and Methods section, cite an entry in the reference list that directs readers there, like this~\cite{methods .}
elements.sent-2411-380: To refer to material in the Supplementary Text section, just write (Supplementary Text).
elements.sent-2411-381: See guidance below for the difference between those two types of supplementary material.
elements.sent-2411-382: Supplementary figures and tables are referred to in lowercase e.g.~figure~\ref{fig:sup_example or table~\ref{tab:sup_example .}}
elements.sent-2411-383: Material in separate files needs to be hand coded e.g.~data~S1, movie~S2.
elements.sent-2411-384: Mathematics: Simple mathematical expressions can be inserted in the text like $2\times3=6$.
elements.sent-2411-385: Variables should be italic but textual labels are roman e.g.~$T_\text{max}$.
elements.sent-2411-386: Explain the meaning of all variables on their first appearance.
elements.sent-2411-387: More complicated expressions should be entered as numbered equations, such as \begin{equation}x=\frac{-b\pm\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}. \label{eq:example}\end{equation} \ Do not indent text immediately after an equation.
elements.sent-2411-388: They can be referred back to as e.g.~Equation~\ref{eq:example .}
elements.sent-2411-389: Formatting: Names of software packages should be set in small capitals e.g.~NumPy.
elements.sent-2411-390: Use a non-breaking space between a number and its unit e.g.~7.4~km, and thin spaces between different parts of a unit e.g.~12~m\,s$^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-391: Use $\pm$ (not parentheses) to indicate uncertainties e.g.~$g=9.8\pm0.2$~m\,s$^{-2}$.
elements.sent-2411-392: Research Articles and Reviews use headings to split the main text into sections; most other formats do not have headings.
elements.sent-2411-393: Length limits: The Science-family journals impose limits on the number of words, figures\slash tables, and references cited in the main text.
elements.sent-2411-394: The limits vary between the journals and article types.
elements.sent-2411-395: Refer to the instructions to authors on the journal website for the current limits.

elements.sent-2411-397: Formatting requirements for each of these sections differ between the Science-family journals; consult the instructions to authors on the journal website for full details.
elements.sent-2411-398: List the grants, fellowships etc. that funded the research; use initials to specify which author(s) were supported by each source.
elements.sent-2411-399: Include grant numbers when appropriate or required by the funding agency.. For example: F.~A.
elements.sent-2411-400: List each author’s contributions to the paper.
elements.sent-2411-401: Use initials to abbreviate author names.
elements.sent-2411-402: Disclose any potential conflicts of interest for all authors, such as patent applications, additional affiliations, consultancies, financial relationships etc. See the journal editorial policies web page for types of competing interest that must be declared.
elements.sent-2411-403: If there are no competing interests, state: ``There are no competing interests to declare.''.
elements.sent-2411-404: Specify where the data, software, physical samples, simulation outputs or other materials underlying the paper are archived.
elements.sent-2411-405: They must be publicly accessible when the paper is published (without embargo) and enable readers to reproduce all the results in the paper.
elements.sent-2411-406: Contact the editor if you’re unsure what needs to be shared.
elements.sent-2411-407: Our preference is for digital material to be deposited in a suitable non-profit online data or software repository that issues the material with a DOI.
elements.sent-2411-408: Alternatively, an institutional repository, subject-based archive, commercial repository etc. is acceptable, as are (short) supplementary tables or a machine-readable supplementary data file.
elements.sent-2411-409: Cite the relevant DOI \cite{dataset , URL \cite{example_url or reference \cite{example2 in this statement.}}}
elements.sent-2411-410: These do not count towards the reference limit if they are only cited in the acknowledgements.
elements.sent-2411-411: Be specific and state a unique identifier -- such as an accession number, software version number or observation ID -- so readers can easily retrieve the exact material used.
elements.sent-2411-412: Declare any restrictions on sharing or re-use -- such as a Materials Transfer Agreement (MTA) or legal restrictions -- which must be approved by your editor.
elements.sent-2411-414: Consult the journal's editorial policies web page for more details.
